title: Spinning a cabled yarn
subtitle: Plying two 2-ply yarns together to produce a round, stable four-strand cable
excerpt: A cabled yarn is made by plying two 2-ply yarns together in the opposite direction to the final ply twist. The result is a round, very stable yarn with high durability. It is slower to produce than a standard 2-ply but is the best structure for socks, hard-wearing garments, and any project that needs a firm, rounded stitch definition.
sourceNotes: Cabled yarn structure documented in traditional hand-spinning practice and in Mary E. Black, The Key to Weaving (1945, in-copyright, substance only referenced) for warp-strength applications. The sock-yarn cabling technique is standard in British spinning guild teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: A yarn structure made by plying two or more plied yarns together, reversing the twist direction at each stage. Each stage adds another layer of twist to the overall structure.
The S or Z direction of the twist applied during plying. To make a cabled yarn, the singles are spun Z, plied S to make a 2-ply, then two 2-plies are plied together Z to make the cable.
The state in which the amount of twist at each stage of cabling exactly cancels out when the yarn hangs free. A balanced cable hangs straight and does not kink or bias.

content.text: requires three spinning passes: spin singles Z, ply two singles S to make two 2-ply yarns, then ply those two 2-plies together Z to make the cable. The
direction reverses at each stage, and
at every stage is what produces the characteristic round, self-correcting cable structure.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Prepared fibre (tops or roving) for four singles
Spinning wheel with at least two bobbins
Lazy kate to hold the 2-ply bobbins during final cabling step

content.text: Spin four bobbins of Z-twist singles. All four should be the same grist (thickness per length) and the same twist level. Inconsistency in any one singles will show as a lumpier or looser section in the finished cable. Sample each bobbin by cutting a 30 cm length and folding it on itself: a properly-twisted singles will ply back on itself smoothly without kinking or opening up.

Ply two singles together with S-twist to make a 2-ply. Ply the other two singles the same way. Both 2-ply yarns should be balanced: a balanced 2-ply hangs straight when cut from the bobbin and held at one end. An overtwisted 2-ply will kink; an undertwisted one will open up. Adjust treadling speed relative to drafting speed until the test length hangs straight.

Place both 2-ply bobbins on the lazy kate. Ply them together with Z-twist, the same direction as the original singles. This is the final cabling pass. The Z-twist at this stage is working against the S-twist of the 2-ply, which is what creates the round, stable cable structure. Use slightly less twist than for a standard plying pass; too much twist at this stage over-cables and makes the yarn stiff.

Wind the cabled yarn onto the niddy-noddy and soak the skein in warm water for 20 minutes. The cable structure is stable but benefits from a good soak to relax the multiple twist layers. Snap gently between your hands while damp, then hang with a slightly heavier weight than you would use for a 2-ply to encourage the round structure to set.

items.problem: The finished cable biases to one side rather than hanging straight
items.cause: One of the singles or 2-ply yarns had a different twist level from the others, creating an imbalance that is amplified through three twist passes
items.fix: Cable yarn is very sensitive to inconsistency in the singles. Respin any bobbin that feels noticeably tighter or looser than the others before cabling.

items.problem: The cable is stiff and wiry rather than round and soft
items.fix: The cabling pass needs less twist than the plying pass. Treadle more slowly or increase the drive-band tension to take up yarn faster relative to wheel rotation.
